stories.headline: City Park bandstand destroyed in overnight fire, investigators call blaze 'suspicious'
stories.summary:
Denver's historic City Park bandstand along Ferril Lake was destroyed in an early-morning fire Thursday, with Denver Fire Department crews arriving at 2:40 a.m. to find the 97-year-old structure fully engulfed in flames. Division Chief Robert Murphy said the bandstand is a total loss and called the overnight fire "suspicious," though investigators are still working to determine the cause.

Built in 1929, the bandstand has hosted generations of community concerts, festivals, weddings, and quinceañeras. It serves as the home venue for City Park Jazz, which is set to celebrate its 40th season this summer, with the opening concert scheduled for June 7 featuring the DJ Williams Band.

City Park Jazz officials said the board of directors is already exploring options to keep the summer season alive and will partner closely with the city. Local advocacy group City Park Friends and Neighbors quickly launched a restoration fund for community members who want to donate to repair or rebuild the structure.

Denver Parks and Recreation said staff will review permits for events scheduled at the bandshell and work with affected groups to determine the best path forward, including rescheduling, relocating, or canceling with a refund.

stories.headline: Murray scores season-high 53, Jokic nearly records historic 20-20-20 game in Nuggets' 142-135 win over Mavericks
stories.summary:
Jamal Murray scored a season-high 53 points and Nikola Jokic posted 23 points, 21 rebounds and 19 assists as the Denver Nuggets defeated the Dallas Mavericks 142-135 on Wednesday night, securing their fourth straight victory and maintaining a half-game lead over Minnesota for fourth place in the Western Conference.

The performance marked a historic milestone: Denver became the first team in NBA history to have one player score 50 points while another recorded at least 15 points, 15 rebounds and 15 assists in the same game. Jokic narrowly missed what would have been an unprecedented 20-20-20 game, notching his 30th triple-double of the season.

Murray, now averaging 25 points per game — by far the most in his career — is making a strong case for his first All-NBA selection. Coach David Adelman said the accolade "should not be an argument." Murray and Jokic have guided Denver through a difficult stretch that included a 16-game absence by Jokic due to a knee injury earlier this season.

With six of Denver's remaining eight regular-season games at home and the full roster finally healthy, the Nuggets are positioning themselves as a serious postseason threat. Jokic needs 29 more assists in the final eight games to average a triple-double for the season, which would be just the seventh time in NBA history a player has done so.

stories.headline: Broncos trade No. 30 overall pick to acquire wide receiver Jaylen Waddle
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have agreed to trade the 30th overall pick in the 2026 NFL Draft, along with third- and fourth-round selections, in exchange for wide receiver Jaylen Waddle and a fourth-round pick, according to ESPN's Adam Schefter. The deal represents a significant shift in Denver's draft strategy as the team prioritizes adding a proven offensive weapon.

Waddle, who is listed as dealing with a rib injury, gives the Broncos a dynamic pass-catcher to complement second-year quarterback Bo Nix, who threw four touchdowns during Denver's 11th straight victory earlier this season. The acquisition signals the franchise's intent to build around Nix with experienced skill-position talent rather than relying solely on the draft.

The trade marks a notable departure from Denver's pre-draft planning, as the team had previously been scouting running backs and other offensive positions ahead of the draft. With the first-round pick now gone, the Broncos will enter the draft without a selection until the third round at the earliest.

Denver's depth chart has shifted considerably since free agency opened, and the Waddle deal continues that trend of roster reconstruction around the young quarterback heading into what the organization hopes is a competitive 2026 season.

stories.headline: Avalanche's Lehkonen returns from upper-body injury as Colorado hosts home-and-home with Jets
stories.summary:
Colorado Avalanche forward Artturi Lehkonen is expected to return to the lineup Friday night against the Winnipeg Jets after missing more than three weeks with an upper-body injury sustained March 3 against the Anaheim Ducks. Head coach Jared Bednar had described the injury as a "weird hit" and initially ruled Lehkonen week-to-week.

Lehkonen has posted 19 goals and 23 assists for 42 points in 59 games this season and is one of the team's most reliable two-way forwards. His return gives Colorado a significant boost as the Avalanche hold the best record in the NHL at 47-13-10 with 104 points, leading the Central Division by seven points over the Dallas Stars.

In Lehkonen's absence, Ross Colton was elevated into a top-six role alongside Brock Nelson and Valeri Nichushkin. With Lehkonen back, the Avalanche can restore their preferred forward depth as they enter the final stretch of the regular season.

Lehkonen also represented Finland at the 2026 Milano Cortina Winter Olympics, where he earned a bronze medal and posted six points in six games. The Avalanche have struggled historically at Winnipeg's Canada Life Centre, making Lehkonen's return all the more important for tonight's road contest.

stories.headline: Woman found dead in Denver home during welfare check; death investigated as homicide
stories.summary:
Denver police discovered a woman dead inside a residence on the 1600 block of Logan Street late Wednesday evening after responding to a welfare check call at approximately 8:14 p.m. Officers found the woman deceased upon arrival and the death is being investigated as a homicide.

The woman's identity and additional details surrounding the incident have not been released by the Denver Police Department as of Thursday. No arrests have been announced and the investigation is ongoing.

Anyone with information about the case is asked to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at (720) 913-7867.

stories.headline: Denver police seek hit-and-run driver after scooter rider struck near downtown
stories.summary:
Denver police are searching for the driver of a black 2018 Nissan Maxima who struck a scooter rider near 15th and Market streets and fled the scene Friday evening. The crash occurred at approximately 8:45 p.m., according to a Medina Alert issued by the Colorado Bureau of Investigation.

The driver is believed to be a white woman with blonde hair who was last seen heading south on Auraria Parkway after the collision. The vehicle, bearing Colorado license plate CYW-Y89, is believed to have damage to the driver's side rear door.

The scooter rider was injured in the crash, though the severity of those injuries was not disclosed. Anyone with information is asked to contact the Denver Police Department at 720-913-6010.

stories.headline: Colorado bill removing statute of limitations for conversion therapy survivors advances through committee
stories.summary:
A bill that would remove the statute of limitations on civil claims brought by survivors of conversion therapy passed a Colorado legislative committee this week. The measure would allow survivors to sue for damages regardless of how much time has passed since the therapy was administered.

Sponsor Rep. Alex Valdez, a Denver Democrat, said the bill recognizes that harm caused by conversion therapy can take years for survivors to process and fully understand. Conversion therapy, which attempts to change a person's sexual orientation or gender identity, is already banned for use on minors by licensed therapists in Colorado.

The bill now advances to the full chamber for further consideration. Advocates say removing the statute of limitations is a critical step in providing legal recourse for LGBTQ individuals who experienced the practice, often as minors, and may only be coming to terms with its effects decades later.

stories.headline: Colorado 115 reopens after week-plus closure due to wildfire on Fort Carson
stories.summary:
Colorado Highway 115, connecting Colorado Springs to Penrose, reopened to traffic Thursday afternoon after being shut down for more than a week due to the 24 Fire, which ignited March 18 when a spark from a malfunctioning vehicle on the roadside ignited dry vegetation. As of Thursday, the fire had burned 7,385 acres and was 61% contained.

Officials warned drivers that smoke may still be visible as interior pockets of the fire continue to burn on Fort Carson land. Travelers were urged to avoid stopping to observe or photograph the fire, and wildlife returning to their habitat are expected to be active near the highway.

No structures were lost or damaged during the fire, and all evacuations that had been temporarily put in place have been lifted. Fire crews will remain in the area to monitor conditions, with the Fremont County Sheriff's Office noting that high temperatures Thursday could increase visible smoke plumes but that containment lines remain intact.

stories.headline: Colorado ski resort closing dates: Several areas shut down early as snowpack crumbles
stories.summary:
Record-low snowpack across Colorado is prompting several ski resorts to close before their previously announced target dates, with more early closings potentially on the way as spring temperatures accelerate snowmelt. Granby Ranch is set to close March 27, followed by Monarch, Purgatory, and Echo Mountain on March 29, while Keystone, Crested Butte, Wolf Creek, and Telluride are slated to close April 5.

Several major resorts, including Ski Cooper, Powderhorn, Buttermilk, and Sunlight, have already closed for the season. Breckenridge and Arapahoe Basin say they plan to stay open as long as conditions permit, while Loveland is hoping to extend into May, though that timeline is in doubt.

Vail is maintaining its April 19 closing date, as is Aspen Mountain and Eldora, while Beaver Creek, Snowmass, Aspen Highlands, and Steamboat are targeting April 12. Winter Park officials say they plan to keep both sides of the resort open through at least April 12 before making further decisions.

The widespread early closures are a significant blow to the state's ski industry, which has faced mounting challenges from the combination of an unusually dry winter and the hottest March on record in the Denver area.

stories.headline: Cold front to bring rain, snow mix to Denver after another near-record warm day Thursday
stories.summary:
Denver is in for a dramatic temperature swing Thursday, with a high of around 85 degrees expected before a cold front moves through in the mid-afternoon, bringing gusty winds and a sharp drop in temperatures. The National Weather Service says the current March 26 record of 84 degrees, set in 1971, could be challenged before conditions deteriorate.

Forecasters say rain and snow showers will spread into the urban corridor and foothills overnight, with some snow potentially mixing down to lower elevations by early Friday morning. A 60% chance of precipitation — described as a wintry mix or snow — is forecast along the I-25 corridor between midnight and 3 a.m. Friday, with a few tenths of an inch of liquid precipitation possible.

Lows Friday morning are expected to fall into the 30s, representing as much as a 47-degree temperature swing in less than 20 hours. The brief cool spell is expected to last only into the weekend, with warmer air and potentially more record-breaking heat forecast to return.

stories.headline: Colorado Lotto+ ticket worth $3.1 million sold at Colorado Springs Safeway
stories.summary:
A winning Colorado Lotto+ ticket worth $3.1 million was sold at a Safeway store in Colorado Springs, lottery officials announced. The jackpot game carries odds of 1 in 3,838,380, according to the Colorado Lottery.

The winner has not yet been publicly identified. Colorado Lotto+ winners typically have 180 days from the drawing date to claim their prize at Colorado Lottery headquarters.

joke.joke: Whenever I'm in a hotel and I hear someone in the next room, I always think, 'I hope they can't hear me eating.'

weekendEvents.title: Denver Summit FC Inaugural Home Match vs. Washington Spirit
weekendEvents.description: Colorado's new National Women's Soccer League team, Denver Summit FC, plays its first-ever home match against the Washington Spirit at Empower Field at Mile High. A halftime performance by Denver electro-funk duo Big Gigantic is scheduled.
weekendEvents.location: Empower Field at Mile High, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Denver Summit FC Inaugural Match Watch Party at McGregor Square
weekendEvents.description: Watch the Summit FC inaugural home match for free on McGregor Square's 66-foot LED Jumbotron. Food options nearby include High Point Creamery, Anthony's Pizza, G-Que BBQ, and Taco Mucho Bueno.

weekendEvents.title: Denver Nuggets vs. Utah Jazz
weekendEvents.description: The Denver Nuggets host the Utah Jazz in NBA regular season action at Ball Arena.

weekendEvents.title: Denver Nuggets vs. Golden State Warriors
weekendEvents.description: The Denver Nuggets host the Golden State Warriors in a late-season NBA matchup at Ball Arena. Tickets start at $46; the game also airs on KOAA-TV.

weekendEvents.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Two Towers – In Concert
weekendEvents.description: The Colorado Symphony, Colorado Symphony Chorus, and soloists perform Howard Shore's complete score live while the film plays on screen. Tickets start at $70.40.

weekendEvents.title: Denver Home & Garden Show
weekendEvents.description: The annual home and garden exhibition at the National Western Complex features home and garden products, window displays, informative presentations, and industry vendors. Adults $13, seniors $7, children under 12 free.
weekendEvents.location: Livestock Center at National Western Complex, 4850 National Western Drive, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Frozen Dead Guy Days
weekendEvents.description: The festival honoring cryogenically preserved Grandpa Bredo Morstoel returns at its new home in Estes Park, featuring coffin races, a polar plunge, an 8K race, a bar crawl, and the Royal Blue Ball. The main festival runs noon to 9 p.m. Saturday at the Estes Park Events Complex; general admission starts at $64.36.
weekendEvents.time: Various; main festival Saturday 12:00 p.m.–9:00 p.m.
weekendEvents.location: Estes Park Events Complex, Estes Park

weekendEvents.title: Spring Vibe Market Denver
weekendEvents.description: A free two-day indoor artisan market at The Arch in RiNo featuring 130-plus local makers, DJ CYN, cocktail bars, food trucks, an Astro Coffee Van, aura photos, and an art gallery. Family and pet friendly.

weekendEvents.title: Black Cube Shorts: A Mini Film Festival
weekendEvents.description: Black Cube Nomadic Art Museum presents screenings of short experimental films commissioned from international artists Julie Bena, Anna Uddenberg, and Alejandro Almanza Pereda, followed by a Q&A with Executive Director and Chief Curator Cortney Stell. Admission is free with RSVP; $5 suggested donation.
weekendEvents.location: Black Cube Headquarters, 2925 S. Umatilla St., Floor 2, Englewood

weekendEvents.title: Special Olympics Colorado Polar Plunge & 5K
weekendEvents.description: A cold-water fundraiser at Aurora Reservoir benefiting nearly 21,000 Special Olympics Colorado athletes, with a goal of raising $726,000. Denver7 chief meteorologist Lisa Hidalgo will emcee.

weekendEvents.title: Center for African American Health 25th Annual Health Expo
weekendEvents.description: A free, public health expo hosted by the Center for African American Health at the DoubleTree by Hilton Denver Central Park, running from 9 a.m. to 2 p.m.
weekendEvents.location: DoubleTree by Hilton Denver Central Park, 3203 Quebec Street, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Cherokee Trail High School Spring Craft Fair
weekendEvents.description: An annual craft fair featuring more than 200 vendors, benefiting Cherokee Trail High School programs.
weekendEvents.location: Cherokee Trail High School, 25901 E. Arapahoe Road, Aurora

topStory.headline: Senate Passes DHS Funding Bill Without ICE Budget, Ending Partial Shutdown of Most Homeland Security Operations
topStory.summary:
The U.S. Senate voted unanimously in the early hours of Friday to approve a funding package for most of the Department of Homeland Security, effectively ending a partial shutdown that had lasted more than 40 days. The bill, passed by voice vote at approximately 2:20 a.m., funds agencies including the Transportation Security Administration, the Coast Guard, FEMA, and the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, but deliberately excludes Immigration and Customs Enforcement and most of Customs and Border Protection.

The exclusion of ICE and CBP funding was the central compromise that broke a weeks-long partisan standoff. Senate Democrats had refused to approve those agencies' budgets without significant reforms to immigration enforcement practices, citing in particular two fatal shootings of U.S. citizens in Minneapolis attributed to federal agents. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer said the agreement represented a firm Democratic position that ICE and Border Patrol would receive no additional funding without reform, while Senate Majority Leader John Thune argued that Democrats' refusal to negotiate sooner had forced an inefficient, piecemeal approach to funding the department.

The shutdown had caused severe disruption at airports nationwide, as roughly 50,000 TSA officers worked without pay since mid-February, leading many to call out of work or resign entirely. Travelers at major airports including Houston reported waits of two hours or more, with some facilities operating at one-third to one-half of normal checkpoint capacity. Hours before the Senate vote, President Donald Trump announced on Truth Social that he would sign an executive order directing newly confirmed Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin to immediately begin paying TSA agents.

Following passage of the DHS funding bill, Sen. Eric Schmitt of Missouri sought unanimous consent to pass a separate measure funding ICE and CBP with $100 billion over 10 years and no new enforcement restrictions. Sen. Andy Kim of New Jersey objected, blocking that measure from advancing. Schmitt and other Republicans signaled they intend to pursue ICE and CBP funding through the budget reconciliation process, which requires only a simple Senate majority rather than the standard 60-vote threshold, bypassing the Democratic filibuster.

The funding package now moves to the Republican-controlled House of Representatives, where its fate remains uncertain. Some conservatives have expressed reservations about separating ICE funding from the broader DHS bill. Speaker Mike Johnson had been in contact with Thune ahead of the vote, and Thune expressed hope Friday that the House would act quickly to open additional portions of the government.
